steps.title: Identify the pattern in exponents
steps.content:
Look at the exponents of $a$ in the table:
  • When $x = 1$: exponent is $1$
  • When $x = 2$: exponent is $5$
  • When $x = 3$: exponent is $9$
The sequence $1, 5, 9$ has differences of $5 - 1 = 4$ and $9 - 5 = 4$. This is arithmetic with common difference $4$.

steps.title: Write the general formula
steps.content:
For an arithmetic sequence with first term $1$ and common difference $4$, the exponent at position $x$ is:
$$1 + 4(x - 1) = 4x - 3$$
Therefore, $f(x) = a^{4x - 3}$.

Verify with table values: $f(1) = a^{4(1) - 3} = a^{1} = a$ ✓
$f(2) = a^{4(2) - 3} = a^{5}$ ✓
$f(3) = a^{4(3) - 3} = a^{9}$ ✓

steps.title: Solve for $k$ when $f(k) = a^{29}$
steps.content:
Using $f(x) = a^{4x - 3}$, set this equal to $a^{29}$:
$$a^{4k - 3} = a^{29}$$
Since $a > 1$, the exponential function is one-to-one, so the exponents must be equal:
$$4k - 3 = 29$$
$$4k = 32$$
$$k = 8$$
